raw_case:
A unique case report revealing the elusive clinical phenomenon of meningitis retention syndrome
Meningitis retention syndrome (MRS) represents a rare condition in which there is meningitis accompanied by urinary retention in the absence of any other neurological symptoms. MRS is usually misdiagnosed as a urinary tract infection due to varied clinical symptoms. It is a self-limiting syndrome that often presents with prodromal symptoms of meningitis. We report a case of MRS in a 26-year-old Indian male who presented clinically with features of meningitis and developed urinary retention during hospitalisation. Fewer than 30 cases have been described and several of them remain undiagnosed due to lack of diagnostic criteria. Any patient with fever and headache which are prodromal symptoms of meningitis followed by retention should be evaluated for MRS.

Highlights

•This is a case on meningitis retention syndrome which is a rare and elusive thing in itself because it presents promoromaly to meningitis with retention of urine and mostly goes unnoticed and undiagnosed due to overlapping symptoms with UTI. This case will increase the horizon of the reader regarding this diagnosis and will trigger clinicians when they will encounter such patients.1

Introduction

Acute urinary retention refers to the sudden inability to voluntarily void and is one of the common emergencies in urological clinics. Common causes of acute urinary retention include benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), constipation, urinary infections, sacral spinal cord diseases (Guillain- Barrè syndrome), aseptic meningitis, and cerebral demyelinating diseases (acute disseminated encephalomyelitis). Rare cases of acute urinary retention as a result of adverse drug reactions or post-surgical consequences have also been reported.1,2 These causes are common when compared to above mentioned neurological causes. Meningitis-retention syndrome (MRS) refers to a distinct presentation of acute urinary retention accompanied by meningitis, without any other neurological symptoms.3 This condition has not been well acknowledged, and hence its prevalence remains undetermined. To date, only 29 cases of MRS have been reported worldwide, and most of them were from Japan.2 Here we report a case of MRS in a 26-year-old Indian male patient.2

Case presentation

A 26-year-old gentleman reported to the Department of General Medicine with the chief complaint of intermittent fever and headache for 5 days, which was associated with generalised weakness and myalgia. Patient also had a history of seizure while coming to the hospital. On general examination, the patient was febrile. All other vitals were within normal range. On systemic examination, terminal neck stiffness was observed. The patient was admitted with suspicion of meningitis. All the blood investigations were within normal range. Malarial parasite fluorescent, and Dengue Rapid test (NS1, IgM, IgG) was also negative.In view of suspected meningitis CSF analysis was done which showed 162 cells/cumm, glucose- 44 mg/dl, protein- 93 mg/dl, Amylase- 30 IU/L, and LDH- 77 IU/L. This was suggestive of pyogenic meningitis. On day one after admission to the hospital, the patient exhibited an episode of seizure and altered sensorium, due to which, he was shifted to the intensive care unit (ICU), and was started on the anti-epileptic medication, antibiotics, steroids and supportive treatment. Since the patient complained of headaches, seizures and altered sensorium magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain with time-of-flight (TOF) was done which showed a T2 hyperintense signal with diffusion restriction in the splenium of the corpus callosum. MRI Angiography of the Brain showed that all the surrounding arteries were normal in course and calibre. Electroencephalogram (EEG) showed generalised slowing, suggestive of diffuse cerebral dysfunction.Patient complained of inability to void on the day 3 of admission. Transurethral catheterization was done using Foley's catheter and 600mL of urine was drained. Elaborated genitourinary examination of the patient revealed normal meatus, prostate was flat, firm, non-tender, with no nodule on palpation. Both bulbocavernosus reflex and cremasteric reflex could not be elicited on focussed neurological examination. Ultrasonography (USG) of the abdomen and pelvis revealed no significant abnormality. Patient was suspected to have UTI related retention but no abnormality was detected in urinalysis and urine culture showed no growth. The patient was started on alfuzosin 10mg. Following improvement in the patient's health status, he was shifted to the ward after 5 days. Later on, gradually the antibiotics and steroids were tapered and the patient was shifted to oral medications. The patient was discharged with a catheter on oral antiepileptics, protein supplements, laxatives and alpha-blockers.At a 2-week follow-up visit, a urine culture was done, which came back negative. A trial void was given, but failed, hence the patient was again catheterized with Foley's catheter and was planned for urodynamic study(UDS). So after 2 weeks urodynamic study was done which revealed underactive detrusor activity with no voiding(Fig. 1, Fig. 2) hence was started on bethanechol 75 mg od and was advised clean intermittent catheterization.However, the patient developed spontaneous voiding in between CIC in the follow-up period. So after 4 weeks, uroflowmetry was done showing voided volume 138ml Qmax flow 20 ml/sec (Fig. 3), which suggested complete recovery from the diseased condition. The patient has been under regular follow-up for 2 months without any complaints. This case report follows the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki. Informed consent has been obtained from participant and will be provided on demand.3

Discussion

We report a case of MRS in a 26-year-old male with fever and headache, who during hospitalisation for pyogenic meningitis, developed urinary retention. MRS was originally described by Sakakibara in 2005.4 The prevalence of MRS is under-reported, which may be because this condition is often misdiagnosed as urinary tract infection, due to its frequent association with abdominal pain. In MRS, there is no evident causative agent, and the specific symptoms and signs of meningitis are often absent. Thus, usually, the predominant presenting symptom is acute urinary retention alone.4 However, in our case, urinary retention developed after 1 week of the prodromal symptoms. Of 29 cases, in 14 cases, urinary retention developed within 1 week of prodromal symptoms.2Based on the previously reported cases, the mean age of occurrence of MRS is 36 years, with a slight male predilection. In our case, the patient was a 26-year-old male. Commonly reported prodromal signs and symptoms include fever (86 %), and headaches (76 %), as was observed in our case, which was the presenting symptoms of the patient. Additionally, generalised weakness and myalgia were reported in our case. Meningeal symptoms were reported in 65 % of the previously reported cases of MRS, in our case we observed neck stiffness.2 Though the exact mechanism of MRS is not completely understood, it is speculated to occur as a result of spinal shock due to inflammation of upper motor neurons of the spinal cord, meningeal irritation, direct viral/bacteria entry, and the onset of acute disseminated encephalomyelitis after viral/bacteria infection.5,6 In our case, no viral etiology was found. It is presumed that bacterial or viral inflammation as well as post-infection inflammatory demyelination results in sacral myeloradiculopathy.7 MRI of the brain and spinal cord usually shows no abnormalities in MRS patients, which distinguishes it from acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM).8 This may be because the majority of cases are treated as meningitis, involving broad-spectrum antibiotics in combination with corticosteroids, and the use of corticosteroids might result in decreased inflammation, which in turn prevents the occurrence of cerebral and medullary lesions, which are characteristic of ADEM.2 However, in our case, an MRI brain with TOF showed a T2 hyperintense signal with diffusion restriction in the splenium of the corpus callosum.In most of the cases of MRS, blood, urine, and CSF cultures are negative, which may suggest the autoimmune nature of MRS, thus supporting the hypothesis that MRS might be a mild form of ADEM, wherein medullary involvement is absent. In previously reported cases, CSF analysis has shown normal to mildly decreased glucose content, increased protein levels, and mild to severe lymphocytic pleocytosis. Sakakibara et al. have reported higher myelin basic protein levels in one patient, but no other cases have reported this parameter.8 However, in our case, CSF glucose and protein levels were within normal range.A urodynamic study done in the majority of previously reported cases revealed an areflexic detrusor, resulting in an inability to contract the bladder for voiding.2 The urodynamic study performed in our case revealed an underactive detrusor with no voiding, due to which the patient was catheterized and bethanechol was given. Some hypotheses proposed for explaining detrusor hypofunction, and subsequent urinary retention in patients with MRS. Lesions of the central nervous system involving the brain or spinal cord may lead to detrusor hypofunction, as observed in cases of transverse myelitis or ADEM.9 However, features of myelitis and encephalitis are not present in MRS patients, and in such patients, neurologic etiology might be responsible for urinary retention. This is supported by the fact that urological abnormalities like infection of the urinary tract, have not been reported in any of the previous cases, including ours.2 Moreover, urinary retention was observed either concomitantly with meningitis or within a few days after meningitis, suggesting a strong chronological correlation. Though the exact site of the lesion remains questionable, meningeal irritation might lead to an initial acute spinal shock that compromises lower urinary tract innervation.4

Conclusion

MRS is a rare disease condition, which is often misdiagnosed as a urinary tract infection. In this condition, meningitis is associated with urinary retention, and hence in patients presenting with fever and headache, MRS should be considered in the differential diagnosis and the patient should be kept under observation, since in most cases, urinary retention develops during the course of the disease, most often within one week. Medications that are helpful or not are still controversial. Although MRS is a self-limiting disease that resolves within a few weeks, in some cases, it may take 3–6 months.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Sajal Gupta: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Kishan Raj K: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Prashanth Adiga K: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 26-year-old Indian male  
- **Chief Complaint:** Intermittent fever and headache for 5 days  
- **History of Present Illness:** Associated generalized weakness, myalgia, and a history of seizures before hospital admission. Developed urinary retention on day 3 of hospitalization.  
- **Past Medical History:** None reported  
- **Personal History:** None reported  
- **Family History:** None reported  
- **Physical Examination:** Febrile, terminal neck stiffness on admission. Normal vitals otherwise. Genitourinary examination revealed normal findings: flat, firm, non-tender prostate, but absent bulbocavernosus and cremasteric reflexes.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **CSF Analysis:** 162 cells/cumm (suggestive of pleocytosis), glucose 44 mg/dL, protein 93 mg/dL, suggesting pyogenic meningitis.  
  - **MRI Brain (with TOF):** T2 hyperintense signal with diffusion restriction in the splenium of corpus callosum.  
  - **MRI Angiography:** Normal surrounding arteries.  
  - **EEG:** Generalized slowing, suggestive of diffuse cerebral dysfunction.  
  - **Urine Culture and Urinalysis:** No growth, ruling out UTI.  
  - **Ultrasonography Abdomen/Pelvis:** No abnormalities detected.  
  - **Urodynamic Study:** Underactive detrusor activity with no voiding.  

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
   - Bacterial or viral meningitis was suspected due to fever, headache, neck stiffness, and CSF findings. Further evidence (seizure, EEG changes) supported meningitis as the underlying condition.  
   - Urinary retention was evaluated for common causes, including urinary tract infection (no evidence on urinalysis/culture), benign prostatic hyperplasia (excluded based on examination), or neurogenic bladder (supportive findings on urodynamic testing).  

2. **Consideration of Rarer Neurological Causes for Urinary Retention:**
   - Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM): Excluded since MRI findings in MRS patients typically lack characteristic medullary or cerebral lesions of ADEM, as seen here.  
   - Guillain-Barré Syndrome: Excluded due to the absence of ascending weakness or significant peripheral neuropathy.  
   - Spinal cord involvement or sacral myeloradiculopathy: Ruled out due to MRI findings and absence of significant spinal symptoms.  

3. **Identifying Meningitis-Retention Syndrome (MRS):**
   - The presence of meningitis followed by acute urinary retention (with negative urological and neurological studies) is characteristic of MRS. Supporting evidence includes:
     - Negative UTI and urine culture.
     - Urodynamic findings of detrusor underactivity.
     - Chronological correlation between meningitis and the onset of retention.
   - MRS was diagnosed based on clinical features, exclusion of other causes, and alignment with previously described cases in the literature.  

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Meningitis Retention Syndrome (MRS). This was supported by the combination of meningitis symptoms (fever, headache, neck stiffness, seizures, CSF findings), the onset of urinary retention, exclusion of other causes, and gradual recovery aligning with the self-limiting nature of the condition.  

---

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaints and history. He's a 26-year-old male with intermittent fever and headache for five days. The associated symptoms are generalized weakness, myalgia, and a history of seizures before admission. Then he developed urinary retention on day 3 in the hospital. 

First, the fever and headache make me think of infections, especially meningitis. The neck stiffness mentioned in the physical exam supports that, as it's a classic sign of meningeal irritation. The CSF analysis shows pleocytosis with 162 cells/cumm, which is elevated. The glucose is 44 mg/dL, which is within normal range (normal CSF glucose is about 2/3 of blood glucose, so if blood glucose is normal, this might be okay), but the protein is high at 93 mg/dL. High protein and pleocytosis typically suggest an inflammatory process in the CSF. The initial thought here is bacterial (pyogenic) meningitis because of the CSF profile, but sometimes viral or other causes can have similar findings. However, the CSF glucose is normal in this case. Bacterial meningitis usually has low glucose, but maybe it's early or partially treated. Alternatively, viral might have normal glucose but usually lower protein. So maybe this is a bit conflicting.

Next, the MRI findings: T2 hyperintense signal with diffusion restriction in the splenium of the corpus callosum. The splenium lesion is interesting. Diffusion restriction indicates acute ischemia or cytotoxic edema. What causes splenium lesions? There's a condition called reversible splenial lesion syndrome (RESLES), which can be seen in infections, seizures, or metabolic disturbances. Also, some viral encephalitis might involve the splenium. For example, Japanese encephalitis or other flaviviruses, but the patient is from India, so maybe Japanese encephalitis is possible. Alternatively, ADEM (acute disseminated encephalomyelitis) could cause such lesions, but typically multifocal. Also, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis is more common post-infectious or post-vaccination.

The EEG shows generalized slowing, which suggests diffuse cerebral dysfunction. That's non-specific but can be seen in encephalitis, metabolic issues, or postictal states after seizures.

Urinary retention and the urodynamic study showing underactive detrusor activity. The absent bulbocavernosus and cremasteric reflexes point towards a neurological cause, possibly involving the spinal cord or brain. The prostate was normal, so it's not an obstructive issue. The urinary retention here might be neurogenic. So, maybe spinal cord involvement or autonomic dysfunction.

Putting this together: The patient has meningitis (CSF pleocytosis, elevated protein) and encephalitis (seizures, EEG changes, splenial lesion). The splenial lesion could be part of an encephalitic process. The combination of meningitis and encephalomyelitis (inflammation of brain and spinal cord) makes me think of certain pathogens. Also, the urinary retention suggests myelitis or involvement of the spinal cord's autonomic pathways.

Infectious causes to consider: Enteroviruses can cause meningitis. Herpes simplex virus (HSV) encephalitis typically affects the temporal lobes, so maybe less likely here. However, other herpes viruses like VZV or EBV can cause encephalitis with different presentations. Tuberculosis is also a possibility in India; TB meningitis often has a more subacute onset, but can present with seizures and CSF findings with high protein and lymphocytic pleocytosis. However, the MRI finding in the splenium is not typical for TB. 

Another thought: Acute bacterial meningitis with complications like cerebritis or abscess? But the MRI findings in the corpus callosum don't fit that as much. 

What about autoimmune causes? Like autoimmune encephalitis (e.g., anti-NMDA receptor), but usually presents with psychiatric symptoms, movement disorders, etc. But the patient has seizures and urinary retention. Also, the presence of fever suggests infectious over autoimmune.

Looking at the urinary retention and absent reflexes: This could be due to acute transverse myelitis, which can be part of a post-infectious or infectious process. Myelitis can be caused by viruses (e.g., enteroviruses, HSV-2, VZV), or autoimmune conditions like neuromyelitis optica (NMO) or multiple sclerosis (MS). However, MRI angiography was normal, and the brain lesion is in the splenium. MS can present with corpus callosum lesions, but the patient's presentation with acute meningitis and myelitis might be more in line with ADEM, which is typically monophasic and post-infectious.

Wait, but the patient's symptoms started with fever and headache, then seizures and urinary retention. ADEM usually occurs after an infection or vaccination, and presents with multifocal neurological deficits. The splenial lesion could be part of ADEM. However, the CSF here shows pleocytosis with high protein, which can occur in ADEM. But ADEM's CSF can also be normal or show mild pleocytosis. The MRI in ADEM usually shows multiple white matter lesions, but sometimes a single lesion. Hmm.

Alternatively, could this be a case of acute encephalomyelitis with meningitis and myelitis, caused by a specific pathogen. In India, Japanese encephalitis is a possibility, but the MRI findings in JE typically involve the thalamus, basal ganglia, and brainstem. Dengue can cause encephalitis and myelitis, and is common in India. Dengue can also cause a transient splenial lesion. Also, Zika virus or chikungunya are possibilities, but I need to think about their typical presentations.

Another angle: The combination of meningitis, encephalitis (with splenial lesion), and myelitis (urinary retention, absent reflexes) could point to a neurotropic virus affecting multiple areas. Enterovirus 71 can cause brainstem encephalitis and myelitis, but the splenium lesion isn't typical. 

Wait, the MRI showed T2 hyperintensity with diffusion restriction in the splenium. Diffusion restriction usually indicates acute infarction, but in the splenium, it's also seen in cytotoxic edema due to excitotoxicity (like seizures), or in RESLES. If the patient had a recent seizure, maybe the splenial lesion is a consequence. But he had seizures before admission, and the MRI was done during hospitalization. RESLES can resolve over time, and is associated with various causes including infections. So maybe the splenial lesion is a secondary effect of the seizures or the infection itself.

But then, the main issue is the meningoencephalomyelitis. The key here is the combination of meningitis (CSF findings), encephalitis (seizures, EEG), and myelitis (urinary retention, absent reflexes). 

Possible infectious agents that can cause all three: 

- West Nile virus: Can cause encephalitis, meningitis, and acute flaccid paralysis (which is a lower motor neuron issue, but maybe not upper motor neuron signs). However, urinary retention could be part of that. 

- Herpes viruses: VZV can cause meningitis, encephalitis, and myelitis. HSV-2 more associated with meningitis, but HSV-1 with temporal lobe encephalitis. 

- HIV: Acute HIV seroconversion can cause aseptic meningitis and various neurological symptoms, but not sure about splenial lesions.

- TB: As mentioned earlier, but the MRI findings aren't typical.

- Japanese encephalitis: As mentioned, but MRI findings more in thalamus.

Another possibility: Scrub typhus, which is endemic in parts of India. Scrub typhus can cause meningoencephalitis, and complications like acute transverse myelitis. It can present with fever, headache, myalgia, and neurological involvement. CSF may show lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein. Also, scrub typhus can cause vasculitic lesions, which might account for the splenial lesion. However, I'm not sure if splenial lesions are typical in scrub typhus.

Alternatively, Lyme disease can cause meningitis and radiculitis, but the patient is in India where Lyme is less common. 

Considering the MRI finding again: Splenial lesion with diffusion restriction. RESLES is often seen in various conditions, including viral infections. For example, influenza, mumps, adenovirus, etc. The presence of such a lesion in the context of an acute infection could be RESLES. But the key is that the patient has signs of meningitis, encephalitis, and myelitis.

Putting this all together, the most likely diagnosis might be a viral meningoencephalomyelitis, possibly due to an arbovirus like Japanese encephalitis or dengue, but the MRI findings don't fit perfectly. Alternatively, enterovirus 71 or another neurotropic virus. Another possibility is acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) following an infection, which can present with multifocal CNS involvement. However, ADEM typically has multifocal white matter lesions on MRI, but sometimes a solitary lesion. The presence of splenial lesion could fit ADEM. 

But the CSF here shows 162 cells, which is more suggestive of a significant inflammatory process. ADEM can have CSF pleocytosis, but usually less than 100 cells. However, sometimes higher. 

Alternatively, the presence of both meningitis and encephalomyelitis might suggest a more specific diagnosis. Another consideration is neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), which can cause transverse myelitis and brain lesions, but typically in specific areas like the area postrema. However, the presence of fever and CSF pleocytosis points more towards infection.

Wait, the patient's urinary retention with underactive detrusor and absent reflexes suggests a lower motor neuron lesion or a spinal cord lesion affecting the conus medullaris or cauda equina. However, the MRI of the abdomen/pelvis was normal, and the spinal MRI wasn't mentioned. Wait, the MRI angiography was normal, but maybe a spinal MRI wasn't done. If there's a spinal cord lesion, such as in transverse myelitis, that could cause urinary retention and absent reflexes. But the MRI of the brain showed splenial lesion. So perhaps a combination of brain and spinal cord involvement.

Infections that can affect both brain and spinal cord: VZV, HSV, enteroviruses, West Nile virus, etc. 

Alternatively, autoimmune conditions like MOG antibody-associated disease, which can present with ADEM-like lesions, myelitis, and brain lesions including in the corpus callosum. MOGAD can cause splenial lesions. The patient's age is 26, which is within the range for such disorders. However, MOGAD usually presents with optic neuritis or transverse myelitis, but can have encephalitis-like presentations. The presence of fever might suggest infection, but autoimmune can have fever as well due to inflammation.

But given the acute presentation with fever, headache, and myalgia, an infectious cause is more likely. The combination of meningitis, encephalitis with splenial lesion, and myelitis (urinary retention) points towards a viral etiology. Considering the geographical location (India), Japanese encephalitis is a possibility, but the splenial lesion is not typical. Dengue can cause neurological complications, including encephalitis and myelitis, and splenial lesions have been reported in dengue. 

Alternatively, the patient could have tuberculous meningitis with spinal involvement (tuberculous radiculomyelitis), but the CSF glucose in TB meningitis is typically low, which isn't the case here (44 mg/dL is normal if blood glucose is around 70-80). Wait, the CSF glucose is 44, which is lower than normal (normal CSF glucose is about 60-80% of blood glucose; if blood glucose is 90, then CSF would be around 60). If the CSF glucose is 44, and blood glucose isn't mentioned, but if blood glucose was normal (say, 80), then CSF glucose of 44 is within normal (60% is 48, so 44 is slightly low but maybe not significantly). So TB meningitis is possible if there's a mild decrease in glucose. However, the protein is 93, which is elevated. TB meningitis typically has a lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and low glucose. So maybe TB is a consideration. However, the MRI findings of splenial lesion are not typical. TB can cause basal meningeal enhancement, hydrocephalus, infarcts, etc. 

Another angle: The combination of seizures and splenial lesion. Seizures can cause transient splenial lesions, but this patient had seizures before admission. However, the lesion might still be related to postictal changes. But the presence of meningoencephalitis suggests that the lesion is part of the infectious/inflammatory process.

Given all these possibilities, perhaps the most likely diagnosis is viral meningoencephalomyelitis, possibly due to an arbovirus like dengue or Japanese encephalitis, with associated splenial lesion and myelitis. Alternatively, ADEM. 

Wait, another possibility is acute HIV infection, which can present with aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, and various neurological symptoms. However, the patient's risk factors aren't mentioned. 

The CSF analysis: 162 cells, which could be lymphocytes or neutrophils. The summary says "suggestive of pyogenic meningitis", which typically has high neutrophils. But if the CSF had predominantly lymphocytes, then viral or TB. The data here isn't clear on the cell type. The mention of "pyogenic meningitis" suggests high neutrophils, but if the CSF has 162 cells with high protein and normal glucose, it could be bacterial, but maybe the patient was partially treated with antibiotics before the LP, leading to a mixed picture. However, without knowing the cell type differential, it's hard to say. If it's lymphocytic, then viral, TB, or fungal. 

Assuming the CSF is lymphocytic (since the glucose isn't very low), then viral meningitis/encephalitis is more likely. Adding the splenial lesion and myelitis, the diagnosis might be ADEM or a specific viral cause. 

Alternatively, the diagnosis could be meningoencephalitis with associated acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (post-infectious ADEM). But the timing here is that the patient presented with 5 days of fever and headache, then seizures and urinary retention. ADEM typically occurs a few weeks after an infection. 

Another thought: The splenial lesion with diffusion restriction could be due to cytotoxic edema from seizures, but the patient's ongoing meningoencephalitis suggests an active infectious process. 

Putting all this together, the most probable diagnosis is viral meningoencephalomyelitis, possibly due to an arbovirus such as dengue or Japanese encephalitis, given the geographic context. Alternatively, if autoimmune, ADEM. But considering the acute presentation with fever and the CSF findings, infectious etiology is more likely. 

Wait, but the urinary retention and absent reflexes point towards myelitis. So, maybe the diagnosis is acute transverse myelitis with concurrent meningoencephalitis. This combination can be seen in certain infections like West Nile virus, VZV, or enteroviruses. Alternatively, it could be part of a post-infectious autoimmune process like ADEM. 

In terms of the MRI finding in the splenium: There's a condition called "MERS" (mild encephalitis/encephalopathy with a reversible splenial lesion), which is often associated with viral infections and presents with transient splenial lesions. The patient's presentation could align with MERS, especially if the encephalopathy is mild. However, the patient had seizures and urinary retention, suggesting more extensive involvement. 

But the presence of urinary retention (myelitis) and splenial lesion (encephalitis) alongside meningitis makes me think of a viral cause affecting multiple areas. 

In the absence of more specific tests (like PCR for viruses, antibody tests), but given the data provided, the most likely diagnosis is meningoencephalomyelitis caused by a viral agent, possibly an arbovirus. However, considering the MRI findings and the urinary retention, another possibility is acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM), which can present with multifocal neurological signs following an infection. 

Alternatively, Japanese encephalitis (JE) is prevalent in India and can cause encephalitis with movement disorders and seizures. However, JE typically affects the thalamus, basal ganglia, and brainstem, which aren't mentioned here. The splenial lesion isn't typical for JE. 

Another possibility is herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE), but HSE usually involves the temporal lobes, which isn't mentioned here. 

Given the ambiguity, but based on the provided data, the most fitting diagnosis would be viral meningoencephalitis with associated acute transverse myelitis. However, given the splenial lesion and the combination of CNS and spinal symptoms, another possibility is ADEM. 

But the CSF findings here (162 cells, high protein) are more suggestive of an active infection rather than a post-infectious autoimmune process. Therefore, the leading diagnosis is likely an infectious meningoencephalomyelitis. However, without specific pathogen identification, it's hard to be certain. 

In the context of the information given, the MRI finding in the splenium could be a clue towards a specific diagnosis. Splenial lesions are seen in several conditions, including ADEM, MERS, and infections like dengue. 

In India, dengue is endemic and can present with neurological manifestations, including encephalitis, meningitis, and rarely myelitis. Some case reports have described splenial lesions in dengue. The patient's symptoms of fever, headache, myalgia, and neurological involvement could fit dengue with neurological complications. 

Alternatively, if the CSF is neutrophilic, then bacterial meningitis with complications. But the CSF glucose is not low enough, and the patient's course is over days, which may not fit acute bacterial meningitis without treatment. 

Given the available data, the most plausible diagnosis is acute viral meningoencephalomyelitis, possibly due to dengue virus, leading to the observed neurological complications including splenial lesion and myelitis. 

However, another angle: The presence of seizures and splenial lesion might suggest a postictal phenomenon (RESLES), but the ongoing meningoencephalitis suggests an active infection. So the splenial lesion could be part of the infectious process or a result of seizures. 

But considering all factors, the leading diagnosis is viral meningoencephalomyelitis. Given the geographic origin and clinical presentation, possible etiologies include dengue, JE, or another arbovirus. However, without specific serology, the best answer based on the provided data is meningoencephalitis with myelitis. But the answer needs to be a specific diagnosis. 

Alternatively, the combination of meningitis (CSF findings), encephalitis (seizures, EEG), and myelitis (urinary retention) points to neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) with area postrema syndrome, but the absence of specific antibodies mentioned and the presence of fever make this less likely. 

Wait, the patient's MRI angiography was normal, which rules out vascular causes. The MRI brain showed splenial lesion. The urodynamic study shows underactive detrusor, which is a lower motor neuron sign. This could indicate a conus medullaris or cauda equina lesion, but the imaging of the abdomen/pelvis was normal. If the spinal cord was imaged and showed a lesion, that would support myelitis. However, the case summary doesn't mention spinal MRI, only brain MRI and angiography. 

In the absence of spinal MRI, but given the clinical signs of myelitis (urinary retention, absent reflexes), it's reasonable to assume spinal cord involvement. 

Putting it all together, the diagnosis is likely acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) presenting with meningoencephalomyelitis. ADEM can present with multifocal neurological symptoms, including encephalopathy, seizures, and myelitis. The splenial lesion on MRI can be seen in ADEM. The CSF in ADEM can show lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein. The history of a preceding infection (though not mentioned here) might be absent or not reported. 

Alternatively, the patient could have a combined bacterial and autoimmune process, but less likely. 

Given the MRI findings and the multisystem CNS involvement, ADEM is a strong possibility. However, the presence of fever might suggest an active infection. But ADEM can present with fever as part of the inflammatory response. 

Another consideration is autoimmune encephalitis, but the patient's presentation includes myelitis and meningitis, which is broader. 

In conclusion, after considering all possibilities, the most likely diagnosis is ADEM with meningoencephalomyelitis. However, I need to cross-check the criteria for ADEM. ADEM typically occurs in children, but can occur in adults. It usually follows an infection or vaccination, has acute onset of multifocal CNS symptoms, and MRI shows multifocal white matter lesions. However, a single lesion in the splenium can occur. The presence of CSF pleocytosis and elevated protein supports this. 

Alternatively, if the splenial lesion is due to a specific infection like dengue or JE, but without more typical findings for those, ADEM is more likely. 

But the patient is from India, where infectious causes are more common. So perhaps the answer is Japanese encephalitis, but the MRI findings don't align. Alternatively, if the splenial lesion is a red herring and the main issue is bacterial meningitis with complications like cerebral edema and neurogenic bladder, but the CSF glucose isn't low enough. 

Wait, the CSF analysis: glucose 44 mg/dL. If the blood glucose was, say, 80 mg/dL, then CSF glucose should be around 48-64 (60-80%). 44 is slightly below that, which could be a mild decrease. But in pyogenic meningitis, the glucose is usually much lower (like <40). So maybe the CSF isn't typical for pyogenic. The description says "suggesting pyogenic meningitis", but maybe that's a misclassification. If the CSF has lymphocytes, then it's more likely viral or TB. 

If the CSF is lymphocytic, then the leading differentials are viral meningoencephalitis, TB meningitis, or autoimmune. 

But given the MRI findings and the clinical presentation, perhaps the best fit is ADEM. However, the presence of fever and acute onset may still lean towards infection. 

Another angle: The combination of splenial lesion and urinary retention. There's a case report of Listeria meningoencephalitis presenting with splenial lesion and urinary retention. Listeria can cause meningitis, encephalitis, and brainstem involvement. However, the CSF in Listeria typically shows pleocytosis with neutrophils, and the patient may have a subacute course. 

Alternatively, the patient might have tuberculosis with atypical presentations, but again, the MRI findings don't align. 

In conclusion, after considering all possibilities, the most likely diagnosis is acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) with meningoencephalomyelitis. However, without a clear history of preceding infection or vaccination, this is uncertain. Alternatively, viral meningoencephalomyelitis, perhaps due to an arbovirus like dengue or JE. 

But given the provided data and the MRI findings, the answer might be ADEM. However, the presence of fever and acute onset could also point to an infectious etiology. Since the user provided that the MRI showed splenial lesion with diffusion restriction and the CSF findings, I need to make a choice. 

Another possibility is autoimmune encephalitis with myelitis, but again, the fever suggests infection. 

In the absence of more specific tests, the answer would hinge on the most probable based on the data. The presence of splenial lesion with diffusion restriction in the context of meningoencephalitis and myelitis could point towards ADEM. Therefore, the final diagnosis is ADEM.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with fever, headache, seizures, urinary retention, and CSF findings (pleocytosis, elevated protein) indicative of meningoencephalitis. The absence of UTI and normal abdominal imaging suggests neurological involvement.  
<step 2> The MRI brain reveals a T2 hyperintense lesion with diffusion restriction in the splenium of the corpus callosum. This finding, combined with generalized EEG slowing, supports encephalitic or post-infectious inflammatory processes like ADEM or RESLES.  
<step 3> Urinary retention, absent bulbocavernosus/cremasteric reflexes, and underactive detrusor activity indicate myelitis. This multisystem CNS involvement (meningitis, encephalitis, myelitis) aligns with ADEM, which often follows infections and causes multifocal demyelination.  
<step 4> While arboviral infections (e.g., dengue, JE) are endemic in India and can cause similar symptoms, the splenial lesion and lack of typical JE MRI features (e.g., thalamic involvement) favor ADEM. CSF findings (lymphocytic pleocytosis, moderate protein elevation) also match ADEM over pyogenic or viral meningitis.  

### Answer:
Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis (ADEM)
